summary: DHH recounts being cold-called by a newspaper subscription salesperson who claimed to offer a 'free' subscription. Having fallen for the same pitch before, he recognized the sales tactics: flattery, getting the mark to say yes, then revealing hidden costs. The 'free' three-month subscription actually cost 300 kroner in 'administrative fees.' He called out the deception in real time, leaving the salesperson stammering. The post is an early example of DHH's distaste for dishonest marketing and manipulative sales rhetoric.
key_points: Sales rhetoric tricks like forced agreement and flattery only work on unsuspecting targets — once recognized, they lose all power
Calling something 'free' while charging 300 kroner in administrative fees is straightforward dishonesty
Past experience with the same scam made him immune to it the second time around
The salesperson's inability to deny the hidden costs when confronted directly exposed the fragility of the pitch
money_quotes: I wasn't special. She was trying to sell me something.
Rhetoric traps, however, are only effective against the unsuspecting (or mentally challenged) victim. Once recognized, they're easily evaded.
Don't tell me that you're not trying to sell me something when you are.
Don't tell me free carries a 300 kroner price tag.
I giggled inside as she scrambled to come up with a way of denying what could not be denied.
key_insight: When companies disguise costs as 'free' offers, the real price is your trust — and once burned, the rhetoric loses all its power.
